g.data-narration: Welcome to Classical Synchronization Problems - essential patterns for managing shared resources in concurrent systems.
g.text: Producer-Consumer • Readers-Writers • Dining Philosophers • Sleeping Barber

g.data-narration: Synchronization prevents race conditions where multiple processes access shared data simultaneously, leading to inconsistent results.
g.text: Without proper synchronization, processes accessing shared resources can cause:

g.data-narration: The critical section is code that accesses shared variables and must not run concurrently. Three conditions ensure correctness.
g.text: Only ONE process in CS at any time
No process in CS? Allow entry

g.data-narration: Semaphores are integer variables with atomic wait and signal operations. Binary semaphores work like mutex locks.
The Producer-Consumer problem manages a bounded buffer. Producers add items, consumers remove them, requiring coordination.
Three semaphores solve this: mutex for mutual exclusion, empty for free slots, full for filled slots.
g.text: wait(empty); wait(mutex); add item; signal(mutex); signal(full);

g.data-narration: Readers-Writers handles data access where multiple readers can coexist but writers need exclusive access.
g.text: 1st: Readers priority (no wait unless writer writing)

g.data-narration: The solution uses read_count to track readers, mutex for its protection, and wrt semaphore for writer exclusion.
Dining Philosophers illustrates deadlock: five philosophers need two chopsticks each, but may all pick left first causing deadlock.
Circular wait causes deadlock: each philosopher holds left chopstick waiting for right, forming an unbreakable cycle.
g.text: All philosophers pick LEFT chopstick simultaneously:
• P0 holds C0, waits for C1
• P1 holds C1, waits for C2

g.data-narration: Three solutions prevent deadlock: limit philosophers, asymmetric picking, or make chopstick pickup atomic with mutex.
The Sleeping Barber models resource scheduling: barber sleeps without customers, customers wait in chairs if available.
The solution uses customers and barbers semaphores for signaling, and mutex to protect the waiting counter.
g.text: while(1){ wait(customers); wait(mutex); waiting--; signal(barber); signal(mutex); cutHair(); }
wait(mutex); if(waiting<chairs){ waiting++; signal(customers); signal(mutex); wait(barber); getHaircut(); }

g.data-narration: Each problem addresses unique synchronization challenges: buffer management, data access, deadlock avoidance, and resource scheduling.
Remember: initialize semaphores correctly, watch for deadlock in resource allocation, and practice implementing solutions.
g.text: ✓ Understand core constraints of each problem
